title: HSBC agrees to $35m fine for scam failure
full_text:
HSBC Australia has admitted it failed to protect customers from being scammed out of their life savings and has agreed to pay a $35 million penalty after being hit with a landmark legal action by the corporate regulator.
The settlement between the Australian Securities and Investments Commission and HSBC was approved by the Federal Court on Thursday, with Judge Elizabeth Bennett agreeing that the penalty was appropriate. "I consider the contraventions to be serious, and that the agreed penalty is within the range of what is appropriate," Bennett said.
ASIC began the legal action against HSBC in December 2024, alleging widespread and systemic failures by the bank in dealing with customers being scammed.
"This is one of the first cases of its kind globally and sends a clear message that protecting customers from scams is a core responsibility of banks," ASIC chairwoman Sarah Court said.
"HSBC's alleged failures left customers more vulnerable to scams, tens of millions of dollars out of pocket and waiting months to find out what had happened to their money."
This included a 51-year-old dental technician who lost $47,000, almost all her life savings; $50,000 lost by a 25-year-old architectural assistant; a couple in their 50s who had $48,000 taken out of their home loan account; and a 41-year-old who lost $50,000.
Delays in investigating and resolving scam reports made the harm worse, Court added.
"ASIC has taken this action to hold HSBC to account, and we're pleased affected customers are now being compensated," she said.
HSBC admitted it didn't have proper controls in place between May 2023 and May 2024, and was aware of scammers claiming to represent HSBC from May 2021.
It said unauthorised transaction reports surged 380 per cent between 2023 and 2024, and investigations took on average 144 days.
Between January 2020 and August 2020, HSBC received more than 1000 reports of unauthorised transactions with a total value of $34.6 million, according to documents filed to the court.
"Customer A said it felt like no one at HSBC Australia could help her. She had to repeatedly visit branches and call for updates. She said she felt extremely frustrated and stressed by the experience," ASIC barrister Paul Liondas, KC, told the court on Thursday.
"Customer B said he felt hopeless and powerless throughout what he considered to be a prolonged investigation process."
HSBC failed in 749 cases to comply with the ePayments code that required investigation results, or requests for extra time, be done within 21 days of a report of an unauthorised transaction.
The bank has paid $21.5 million in compensation to customers, and will pay more. It has recovered $6.5 million of the stolen funds and returned them to customers.
"We apologise to our customers who were impacted by these events," an HSBC spokeswoman said. "We are pleased to have reached an agreement to resolve the proceedings with ASIC, which recognises our customer redress program and the significant enhancements made to our fraud and scam prevention, detection and response."
So far this year, Australians have reported 61,400 scams and a total loss of $94.5 million, according to the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission's Scamwatch.
More than half of that was lost to investment scams, which lure people to put money into non-existent financial products. Bank impersonation scams, where a criminal gains access to a customer's account details by claiming to be a bank official, are also on the rise.
The ACCC estimates about a third of scams go unreported because victims are often too embarrassed to come forward.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Max Mason
heading: Banking and Economy
